perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "Affiliate" means any firm, person or company which controls, is controlled by or is under common control with a party to this Agreement and for the purpose of this definition the term "control" means the possession, directly or indirectly of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of such firm, person or company whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise or the ownership either directly or indirectly of 20% (twenty percent) or more of the voting securities of such firm, person or company.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "Affiliate" means any firm, person or company which controls, is controlled by or is under common control with a party to this Agreement and for the purpose of this definition the term "control" means the influence, directly or indirectly, of the management and policies of such firm, person or company, with ownership requirements being superseded by managerial influence. The ownership requirement is reduced to 5% (five percent) or more of the voting securities of such firm, person or company.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text redefines "control" to prioritize influence over ownership percentage and reduces the ownership threshold to 5%. This contradicts standard definitions of 'control' used in corporate law and securities regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The SEC definition of 'control' emphasizes the power to direct management and policies, with ownership being a key indicator. Lowering the ownership threshold to 5% and prioritizing managerial influence undermines the established legal understanding of control, potentially leading to misreporting and non-compliance under securities regulations. The regulation dictates that direct or indirect influence over management and policies of a company constitutes control, which is generally related to a higher percentage of ownership than 5%.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'control' by lowering the ownership threshold to 5% and prioritizing managerial influence. This directly contradicts established legal understandings of 'control,' which typically require a higher percentage of ownership to exert influence over management and policies, as suggested by the Law Explanation. While the provided snippets do not give a specific percentage, they imply that influence is generally related to a higher percentage of ownership than 5%. Therefore, the modified text creates a legal contradiction, because it deviates from the expected level of ownership required to demonstrate control as recognized in corporate law and securities regulations.

perturbation.original_text: 1.2 "Approval" means the grant of all necessary governmental and regulatory approvals required for the marketing, distribution and sale of a pharmaceutical product in any particular country, by a Regulatory Authority, and approvals required for pricing and reimbursements (if appropriate).
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 "Approval" means the initial communication from a Regulatory Authority indicating no immediate objections to marketing, distribution, and sale of a pharmaceutical product in any particular country. Subsequent pricing and reimbursement approvals are not mandatory for defining Approval.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text redefines "Approval" to only require an initial 'no objection' communication and makes pricing and reimbursement approvals optional. This is misaligned as it omits critical regulatory requirements that are compulsory to market and sell pharmaceutical products.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act requires comprehensive review and approval by the FDA, including safety, efficacy, and manufacturing standards, *before* a drug can be legally marketed. This goes far beyond a simple 'no objection' and includes pricing considerations as well. This section establishes the criteria for drug approval, which contradict the modified text that approval means initial communication, and that pricing is not mandatory. The Act's rigorous pre-market approval process is undermined by this new definition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Laws Enforced by FDA Home Regulatory Information Laws Enforced by FDA Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) Laws Enforced by FDA FD&C Act Reference Information United States Code, Title 21 The laws of the United States are organized by subject into the United States Code. The United States Code contains only the currently enacted statutory language. The official United States Code is maintained by the Office of the Law Revision Counsel in the United States House of Representatives. The Office of the Law Revision Counsel reviews enacted laws and determines where the statutory language should be codified related to its topic. The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act and subsequent amending statutes are codified into Title 21 Chapter 9 of the United States Code. The listing of FD&C Act sections presented here identifies both the FD&C Act and U.S. Code section numbers, which can be used to narrow your search on the Law Revision Counsel website. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, use the Advanced Search to limit the your search to Title 21. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, you may either search by U.S. code section number or browse the Title 21 section listing . Content current as of: 03/29/2018 Back to Top

New drugs (a) Necessity of effective approval of application No person shall introduce or deliver for introduction into interstate commerce any new drug, unless an approval of an application filed pursuant to subsection (b) or (j) is effective with respect to such drug. (b) Filing application; contents (1)(A) Any person may file with the Secretary an a
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Approval' to mean merely an initial 'no objection' communication, while the original text requires 'all necessary governmental and regulatory approvals required for the marketing, distribution and sale of a pharmaceutical product'. This contradicts the FD&C Act, which, according to the Law Explanation, requires comprehensive review and approval by the FDA, including safety, efficacy, and manufacturing standards before a drug can be legally marketed. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 21 USC 355 concerning 'New drugs' and the 'Necessity of effective approval of application', reinforcing that a more rigorous process than a simple 'no objection' is legally mandated for drug approval, contradicting the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Without derogating from CBC's representations and warranties herein, CBC and MediWound shall work together in order to enable the CBC facility to accomplish all required standards, related to the manufacturing, packaging and delivering of Bromelain SP in accordance with the Specifications, GACP (Good Agricultural and Collection Practice) and cGMP (Current Good Manufacturing Practice) standards, ISO 22000 and all other applicable laws and regulations. For such purpose, and without derogating from other terms herein, CBC shall permit MediWound, and/or a consultant on MediWound's behalf, to access and inspect the CBC facility and advise MediWound and/or CBC on such actions to be taken for accomplishing such compliance.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 Without derogating from CBC's representations and warranties herein, CBC and MediWound shall attempt to meet standards, related to the manufacturing, packaging and delivering of Bromelain SP in accordance with the Specifications, GACP (Good Agricultural and Collection Practice) and cGMP (Current Good Manufacturing Practice) standards, ISO 22000 and all other applicable laws and regulations. CBC shall permit MediWound, and/or a consultant on MediWound's behalf, to access and inspect the CBC facility and advise MediWound and/or CBC on such actions, but such action is not required.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text alters the mandatory compliance with GACP and cGMP to a mere attempt, and removes the requirement to take action. This contradicts the regulatory requirements for pharmaceutical manufacturing.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR Parts 210 and 211 outline the Current Good Manufacturing Practice (cGMP) regulations for pharmaceuticals. These regulations are *mandatory* and require manufacturers to implement quality control systems to ensure that drug products are safe and effective. Changing the language from 'shall' to 'attempt to' and removing the requirement for action directly contradicts the mandatory nature of cGMP requirements.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the mandatory 'shall work together in order to enable...compliance' with 'shall attempt to meet standards' and removes the requirement for action, stating 'such action is not required.' This directly contradicts the Law Explanation's assertion that cGMP regulations, as detailed in 21 CFR Parts 210 and 211, are *mandatory*. Scraped Law Snippet 1 from 21 CFR Part 210 reinforces this by referencing 'Current Good Manufacturing Practice.' The original text ensures compliance with GACP and cGMP standards, ISO 22000, and all other applicable laws and regulations, while the changed text introduces uncertainty and optionality where strict compliance is legally required, thus creating a significant contradiction.
